meta.name: PPS Pure Procrastination Scale
meta.description: The PPS is a 12-item scale focused on measuring pure procrastination behavior, free from confounding factors. It is one of the most recommended measures in current procrastination research.
meta.author: Steel (2010)
meta.reference: Steel P. Arousal, avoidant and decisional procrastinators: Do they exist? Personality and Individual Differences. 2010;48(8):926-934.
meta.instruction: Please rate how true each statement is of your typical behavior.

questions.text: I delay making decisions until it's too late
Even after I make a decision I delay acting upon it
I waste a lot of time on trivial matters before getting to the final decisions
In preparation for some deadlines, I often waste time by doing other things
Even jobs that require little else except sitting down and doing them, I find that they seldom get done for days
I often find myself performing tasks that I had intended to do days before
I am a person who has a 'I'll do it tomorrow' habit
I generally delay before starting on work I have to do
I find myself running out of time
I don't get things done on time
I am not very good at meeting deadlines
Putting things off till the last minute has cost me money in the past

ranges.label: Low Procrastination
ranges.description: Your procrastination tendency is low. You have good time management and task completion abilities. This indicates that your overall mental health is in good condition, with all areas of functioning working well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your good action-oriented habits
Share your efficiency strategies with others
Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle and mental well-being
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences
Maintain regular exercise and social activities

ranges.label: Moderate Procrastination
ranges.description: You show some procrastination tendencies that occasionally affect your efficiency and ability to complete tasks on time. Mild psychological concerns are common in life. Focusing on self-care and healthy coping strategies can help maintain well-being.
suggestions: Create a daily priority list
Use the 'two-minute rule' — if it takes less than two minutes, do it immediately
Reduce barriers to starting tasks (e.g., prepare your work environment in advance)
Focus on self-care with good routines and regular exercise
Learn simple relaxation and stress-relief techniques

ranges.label: High Procrastination
ranges.description: Your procrastination behavior is quite noticeable, frequently causing delays on tasks and deadlines. At this level, psychological concerns may be affecting your daily life. It is recommended to take active steps and consider available support options.
suggestions: Consider learning time management techniques
Establish an accountability system with friends or colleagues
Try cognitive-behavioral strategies to combat procrastination thinking
Consider consulting a counselor
Consider consulting a mental health professional

ranges.label: Severe Procrastination
ranges.description: Your procrastination problem is very serious, significantly impacting your quality of life and sense of self-efficacy. Your mental health concerns are notable and may be impacting daily functioning. Speaking with a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Recommend seeking professional counseling support
CBT and ACT are effective treatments for procrastination
Start with 'tiny habits' to rebuild your capacity for action
Address the emotional issues behind procrastination (e.g., anxiety, perfectionism)
Schedule a mental health professional assessment as soon as possible
